content:
Hi drlarry, What you're describing sounds like signatures.

From the article:

"Signatures" are blocks of text that are automatically appended to every message that you send (including both new messages and replies to incoming messages). They are generally used to provide additional contact information, legal terms or some other boilerplate information that is relevant to every email.

For more information and instructions on how to add them, please check: Signatures

Yes, it is. Those are known as "signatures". Here's an article about them and how to set them up:

content: If I may offer a suggestion, if your intent is that the recipient focus on content and not on font, use font 'variable=width' and size medium in your settings>composition pane. That setting ensures that your message will be presented in the recipient's font and font size.. As you stated, the other settings in latin and other writing systems are for you, not the recipient. For your signature, also do not use a font or font size. That ensures that your sig will be consistent to recipient.
creator: davidsk

content:
Hey david, thanks for your answer!

I'd say that both font and content are important for me.

Do you know what is actually meant by Variable Width in the HTML style of the composition settings? If I would choose Arial – which should be a system font available across operating systems, right? – my recipients would read the mail in this font? When choosing Arial, I get confused because the font drop-down menu changes from displaying Arial to sans-serif,Arial as soon as I start typing some text. Same question: What is meant with sans-serif here?

Removing the font-family and font-size from my signature HTML solved the style mismatch! Thank you so much!

Have a wonderful day
creator: me511

content: Variable-width is a code word in thunderbird to NOT SEND font style. The lets the recipient see mail in recipients preference. To my knowledge, no other email client allows this freedom. When you see 'sans serif,arial, that is a signal to the recipient's email client to use a similar font to arial if arial is not on the PC. The good news in setting the HTML compose option to variable-width and medium font is that the recipients see your message in their desired font and font size. A win/win.. I was glad to assist and pleased that the sig now works properly. :)
creator: davidsk

content:
Thanks to Ed & sfhowes for your helpful replies. It was "sfhowes" solution that solved the problem. I went onto the outlook website, clicked the gear setting icon> Mail>Forwarding and IMAP> Pop and IMAP and selected LET DEVICES AND APPS USE IMAP.

When you first go to Forwarding and IMAP, you have to click a button that says sign in to make changes. I clicked on that and nothing happened. It turns out, I had a "Popup blocker extension" enabled in EDGE. It was preventing the sign-in password popup from opening which prevented me from seeing the "Let Devices and Apps use Imap" toggle switch. I turned off the popup blocker, and the security password popup appeared. I signed in, and that allowed the "Let Devices and apps use Imap" to appear. Toggle on.

So thanks sfhowes, and I hope this detailed solution helps others! This is why I love the internet...people helping people. You guys are great.

content:
Webmail accounts do offer out of office/on holiday types of response, but you would need to enable it.

The only way Thunderbird could it is if you did the following:

  1. created a Template email with message
  2. created a Message Filter which basically does this - after the Junk classification has run (you do not want to auto reply to spam) - when you get an email, it auto replies using the template you created.
  3. Left your computer switched on and logged on to user account and left Thunderbird running. Computer would need to stay awake.

That's not exactly the best course of action as it's unsafe to leave it running logged on etc unattended for long periods.

Strongly advise it's not wise to tell people you are on holiday as it may indicate your home is vacant/unoccupied and an easy target.

content:
I'm certain that that's not a Thunderbird-generated warning. I have never seen anything that looks like that, and just the appearance and wording do not look or sound like Thunderbird. So, the question is, whose is it?

The first thing I'd question is your anti-virus program, assuming you use one. What is it? Does it have some spam/scam detection/warning features that you can turn off or indicate that these messages are trusted (if you do trust them - they look a suspicious to me.)

Who is your email provider? Is yours a POP3 or IMAP account? If POP3 and you're set up to leave messages on the server, or if IMAP, use your email providers web mail interface to see of the warning is present on the server's copy. That would indicate that the warning is placed there by them. Perhaps they have a scam warning feature that you can turn off or control its settings?

The more I look at that warning, the less I trust beermenus. They are either surreptitiously tracking you or it's not really them and they are being spoofed. The "track.pstmrk.it" site appears to be a tracking site in Italy. And what do that Russian River and Firestone Walker sites have to do with beer menus? I would be inclined to block them entirely unless I really had to know what beers were on the menu.
creator: rednoise

content:
Can you show us a screen shot of the warnings? That might help identify the source of the warnings.

Thunderbird does have a feature that is supposed to warn you of suspicious messages and links. That setting is found in <Settings | Privacy & Security | Security | Scam Detection>. Is that option enabled for you?

Here's an article that talks about the feature and how it works. It refers to an older version of Thunderbird and things look a little different now, but they work basically the same.

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/thunderbirds-scam-detection.

If this is not what you're seeing, I would suspect your anti-virus program.

content:
Accounts information will not transfer from your Android device to your PC. If this is your initial install of Thunderbird on the PC, you will have to set up each account from scratch, though, of course, you can compare the settings with those on the Android device. It is asking for your email account user IDs and passwords.

"Accounts Hub" is new, 'experimental' feature that can help you set up email accounts. It works for most people, but is 100% reliable for some. That's why it can be disabled and you can use the old accounts setup wizard, or enter the server information manually. The feature's setting can be found at <Settings | General | Account Hub>. If it's not working for you, you can disable it here.

content:
OK, try this.

Open your profile folder:

- - TB menu > Help > Troubleshooting Information

- click 'Profiles' near the bottom > about:profiles > Local Directory > click 'Open Folder' button

- close TB > rename the handlers.json file in your profile folder

- restart TB > go to TB menu > Help > Troubleshooting Information > click the 'Clear startup cache' button

After restarting, when you click a link in a message, you should be presented with a screen where you can choose which browser to use.

content:
Try this.

Open the config editor:

- TB menu > Settings > General > scroll all the way down > click the 'Config editor' button

Search for and set these to true:

- network.protocol-handler.warn-external.http

- network.protocol-handler.warn-external.https

content: They don't look aligned here, either. Which illustrates my point: I bet you were composing an HTML-format message using a variable-width font, which, as implied by its name, cannot predictably align, since each letter may be a different width. To have a better chance of things aligning the way you want, use a Fixed-Width font, typically Courier. You can change fonts on the fly with the formatting toolbar in the message compose window, and you can change the default font in <Settings | Composition | HTML Style.>

content:
Hello there mikael.k.adolfsson



Great to hear you’ve been a long-time Thunderbird user! Transitioning to Thunderbird for Android involves a specific trade-off between Google’s ecosystem features and your local data privacy.



1. Data Residency & AI Training (Server-Side)You are correct regarding the backend: as long as your data resides on Gmail’s IMAP servers, it is subject to Google’s Terms of Service.Scanning: Google’s automated systems scan incoming/outgoing mail for indexing, spam filtering, and security.

AI Training: For personal (free) Gmail accounts, Google uses certain 'Smart Features' data to improve its models. Using Thunderbird does not stop Google from processing data that is already on their servers.

The Benefit: By using Thunderbird, you avoid providing additional telemetry. The Gmail app collects extensive metadata (usage patterns, device IDs, location), whereas Thunderbird is open-source and does not engage in this type of client-side data mining.


2. Security Features (Server-Side vs. Client-Side)Pre-delivery Filtering: You won't miss the core security. Google’s Spam and Phishing filters run at the server level. By the time Thunderbird fetches your mail via OAuth2/IMAP, malicious emails have often already been flagged or moved to the 'Spam' folder by Google.

Thunderbird’s Protection: Thunderbird provides its own layer of security by blocking remote content (like tracking pixels) by default. This prevents senders from knowing if or when you opened an email—a feature often bypassable in the standard Gmail web/app view.


3. Why it improves Privacy (The "Tracker-Free" Client)Zero Telemetry: The primary privacy gain is moving from a proprietary, data-collecting client (Gmail App) to a privacy-first, open-source client.Encapsulation: Thunderbird acts as a neutral gateway.

If you eventually decide to migrate to a privacy-centric provider (like Proton or Mailbox.org), you can manage everything in one local environment without Google seeing your interactions with those other accounts.
creator: Googlethunderbird.

content:
If you still have a problem then post the following information:

re :I have verified ALL server settings for each account as correct.

That does not necessarily mean you did it correctly. Sorry if I sound as if I do not believe you, but I've had plenty of people believe they checked and I found a discrepancy. So can you do the following because this is the only correct method when dealing with various accounts/email addresses including where there are more than one account using same server settings.

In Thunderbird

  • Account Settings
  • In Left pane - scroll to the bottom and below 'Local Folders' will be 'Outgoing Server (SMTP)' - click on that 'Outgoing Server (SMTP)' and the right pane will display a list of all the Outgoing SMTP servers.

You should have one smtp for each email address.

  • Select the top one and click on 'Edit'
  • 'Description': please type the full email address - it should be identical to the 'Username' email address.
  • click on 'OK'
  • Select the second smtp in list and click on 'Edit'
  • Please edit the 'DEscription' field as advised previously. Obviously, each DEscription will be unique and match whatever email address is in that smtp Username' field.
  • Work down through all the smtp's.
  • If you find an old smtp in list for an email address/account that does not exist - please delete it.

Now you need to check each account to make sure it really is using the correct smtp server.

In Left Pane:

  • Select the name of the account which is usually the email address.
  • Look bottom right for the 'Outgoing Server (SMTP)'

This is where the 'Description' becomes helpful. Does it say the same identical email address as the account name you selected ? If yes, then all is ok. If no, then do the following:

  • At end of DEscription text - Click on the drop down chevron icon to see drop down list and select the correct description where email address matches the account name email address.

DO NOT click on 'Edit SMTP_Server' button - if things are not correct then you end up editing the wrong smtp.

Do the same process for each mail account. In Left pane select the name of account, look bottom right and check the Outgoing Server is displaying the email address of the selected account. Correct as required. Work down through every account.

Then Exit Thunderbird. It will update important files. After a few moments restart Thunderbird

Now post the following information: In Thunderbird

  • Help > Troubleshooting Information
  • Scroll down to 'Mail and News Accounts' section
  • Copy all the data in that section and paste it into this forum question OR upload an image file showing the info.
creator: Toad-Hall

content:
What is your operating system? I will assume Windows. Two things I would try:

- Restart Thunderbird in Troubleshoot Mode (see <Help | Troubleshoot Mode>). This will temporarily disable all add-ons, in case one of them is misbehaving. Now try deleting the accounts.

- See this conversation about a similar situation. The cause had to do with Thunderbird being originally installed under an operating system Administrator account, but the current user only had regular user permissions. Perhaps you are in a similar position.

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/questions/1344354
creator: rednoise

content: OK, tried both of those but neither worked.

content:
Hello there Gjveerman.



The folders might be corrupted, or the index file itself is broken. Are they still visible?No? It might help to run the 'Repair Folder' function on the Inbox folder.This is the fastest way to recover them if the folders exist but are simply not being displayed.



Here’s how Files might be collapsed. Click the '>' or '+' icons next to the Inbox in the left pane to expand the folders.

The next step is to verify your IMAP folder subscriptions. Right-click your email account, select 'IMAP Folders' (or 'Subscribe'), and click 'Query'. Locate the missing folders in the list and ensure they are subscribed to restore visibility.


Here’s how 2 Right-click on Inbox > select Properties. Click the Repair Folder button and then click OK. Thunderbird will now rebuild the index.Did anything specific happen recently that might have caused this?
creator: Googlethunderbird.

content: Normally if you delete folders or subfolders in Thunderbird they would be moved to the Trash folder, in your case the Trash folder in Local Folders. Did you search the Trash folder? If they are no longer in Trash you could retrieve them from a profile backup, if you have one...
creator: Mapenzi

content: check the trash folder.. If it has not been compressed, they may still be available. You can check from File Explorer to see size of the file. Let us know.

content:
Hello there


Very recognisable . The Thunderbird autoconfig (and the Flatpak sandbox) tends to be 'too smart' for its own good, overwriting values as soon as you switch fields—it really is a game of 'whack-a-mole.

The solution that works for me:Click on 'Manual Configuration'.Fill in the Port and SSL settings first (at the bottom).Only then fill in the server names and usernames (at the top).If you set the ports last, Thunderbird often triggers a 're-calculation,' which causes the server settings to jump back to their defaults.Regarding the blank error message: this is often a permissions issue within the Flatpak sandbox, preventing Thunderbird from properly displaying or writing the specific error string.

Tip Tip for SMTP: Check under Account Settings -> Outgoing Server (SMTP) to ensure the port isn't stuck on 25 (it usually needs to be 465 or 587 with SSL/TLS).

Could you please provide the information previously requested?
creator: Googlethunderbird.
